data.source: Laboratory experiments with undergraduate student participants
data.wave_or_subset: Study 1: 62 usable participants (after exclusions) in repeated-measures design; Study 2: 65 usable participants (after exclusions) in repeated-measures design
data.notes: Study 1 originally recruited 72, Study 2 originally recruited 84; exclusions were due to incomplete data or failure to understand instructions.

method.description: Two laboratory experiments tested how outcome variance in a public-goods dilemma affects contribution behaviour. Study 1 used a four-person step-level public-goods game manipulating outcome variance (none, low, medium, high) and provision threshold (low, medium, high). Study 2 used assurance-type public-goods games with dichotomous contributions, manipulating outcome variance (certain gain, uncertain gains-only, uncertain losses-and-gains) and group size (2 vs 8).
steps: Recruit undergraduate participants and seat individually in the laboratory.
Provide written instructions describing the public-goods game and bonus structure.
Administer comprehension quiz and clarify any misunderstandings.
For each game condition, have participants allocate points either to a shared account (contribution) or personal account (keep).
After each decision, collect self-reported strategy/motivation and manipulation-check items.
Randomly match participants’ decisions ex post to determine pay-offs; pay one randomly selected decision at 1 point = $0.01.
Analyse data: Study 1 – repeated-measures ANOVA; Study 2 – Generalised Estimating Equations logistic regression.
method.models: Study 1: Repeated-measures ANOVA; Study 2: Generalised Estimating Equations (logistic regression)
method.outcome_variable: Study 1: Number of points contributed to the shared account; Study 2: Binary cooperation decision (1 = contribute 125 points, 0 = do not contribute)
method.independent_variables: Outcome variance/prospect type; provision threshold (Study 1); group size (Study 2)
method.control_variables: Not explicitly labelled as controls; within-subjects design inherently controls for individual differences.

results.summary: Across both experiments, outcome variance reduced contributions only when the distribution of possible outcomes included loss prospects. With gains-only prospects, contributions were similar to the no-variance condition. In Study 1, mean points contributed dropped when losses-and-gains were possible. In Study 2, cooperation rates fell sharply in the losses-and-gains condition. Larger groups also exhibited lower cooperation, replicating prior findings.

metadata.original_paper_title: Is it even worth it? The effect of loss prospects in the outcome distribution of a public goods dilemma
